RT by @NotWoofers: Russian refinery throughput has now collapsed to 2.8mbpd from 5.1mbpd in 2022. Omsk is severely damaged, though not yet fully offline (see below). Gasoline, diesel and jet shortages are now emerging simultaneously. With multiple crude distillation units (CDUs) knocked out across the refinery system, there is no quick path to restoring refining capacity. Sustained product imports are becoming Russia's only viable medium-term solution. Good luck with that. Belarus' Mozyr refinery is the Kremlin's best hope for securing up to 90kbpd of gasoline and a bit of jet and diesel. But it sits just across the Ukrainian border and is itself an obvious strategic target - expressis verbis by @ZelenskyyUa. @MFA_Ukraine

The headlines here are a bit uncharitable. Mr. Pavel’s point though is he believes Putin will strongly consider mobilization after parliamentary elections, thus Ukraine has a narrow window of opportunity to exploit. Honestly though I don’t think a mobilization will do much to solve the Russian problem of not being able to mass forces. RT by @NotWoofers: EXCLUSIVE: Russia still has “considerable firepower and considerable potential” in its air and missile forces, NATO’s top general told me, particularly if those weapons are paired with Russia’s large number of one way attack drones. “This kind of combined aerospace attack, if you will, is something that we’re going to have to be ready to counter,” NATO’s Supreme Allied Commander Europe Gen. Alexus Grynkewich said in a recent interview with >@ASForcesMag. Grynkewich said the Russian Aerospace Forces “have not been subject to the same level of attrition and attritional warfare that the Ukrainians have imposed on Russian ground forces. The platforms are there, and then their production for replacement ballistic missiles and cruise missiles continues.”
